items.title: Everyone is long momentum now — and the exit door is the same as the entrance
items.idea: The rally has become so reflexive that every structural bid is now also a potential structural offer: overwriters forced to cover, levered ETF daily rebalances, underweight discretionary funds capitulating. Lord Fed's Phase 3 basket (effectively short momentum) is alive and showing accumulation in software names — but the key warning is that when reflexive bids reverse, the speed of the move equals the speed of the bid.
items.angle: Positioning crowding is the hidden risk: the same machinery that made the rally clean makes the reversal clean. The short-vol feedback loop from overwriters and levered ETFs is the tell.
items.tweetA: Buried in today's note: every single bid that made this rally clean is also reflexive on the way down. Overwriters forced to roll up. Levered ETF AUM rebalancing daily in the direction of price. Discretionary funds capitulating and buying late. Same door, opposite direction. The rally built its own circuit breaker.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this: the best time to worry about a market is when the last bears fold. Not because they're always right. But because you just lost your natural buyers. The discretionary cohort is now long. Who's left to buy the next dip?

items.title: US 30yr Treasury above 5.10% — 10yr coiling for breakout, global yield compression ending
items.idea: The 30yr Treasury yield is back above 5.10%, pressing against upper bounds of a multi-year range and threatening fresh cycle highs. The 10yr is resolving higher from a textbook coil pattern — which typically precedes sharp directional expansion. Both yields have been in tight ranges near multi-decade highs, which historically means the volatility expansion when it comes will be rapid and cross-asset.
items.angle: Alfonso De Pablos calls the 30yr chart 'probably the most important chart in the world right now.' If the coil breaks up, rates vol will cascade into equities, commodities, and currencies simultaneously.
items.tweetA: The 30yr Treasury is above 5.10%. The 10yr is coiling. Alfonso at All Star Charts calls it the most important chart in the world right now. He's not wrong. When these ranges break, they don't drift — they snap. Everything from equity multiples to mortgage rates reprices overnight.
items.tweetB: Bond vol has been suppressed for years. Tight ranges near multi-decade highs. All Star Charts today: that suppression is ending globally. 30yr at 5.10%+, 10yr coiling. The expansion doesn't stay in bonds — it hits stocks, commodities, currencies. All of them at once.

charts.desc: Chart of 10yr and 30yr Treasury yields showing multi-year range with coil formation — key technical setup. CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: AI Stuxnet: 20-year-old computer virus selectively sabotaged precision engineering calculations
items.idea: SentinelOne investigation uncovered fast16.sys, a virus that patches high-precision calculation software in memory to introduce systematic errors — targeting LS-DYNA 970 (cited in reporting on Iran JCPOA nuclear violations), PKPM, and MOHID. Unlike typical malware, it injects a standalone floating-point math function to corrupt results in civil engineering, physics, and physical process simulations. This predates Stuxnet by 5+ years.
items.angle: The AI-nuclear parallel is genuinely disturbing: Import AI's Jack Clark flags this as a preview of how a superintelligence might pursue AI non-proliferation — subtle degradation of a rival's scientific capability rather than direct attack.
items.tweetA: A computer virus from 2000-2005 has been discovered that doesn't steal data or crash systems. It quietly corrupts physics calculations — crash simulations, structural analysis, nuclear weapons modeling — introducing small systematic errors across an entire facility. Stuxnet but 5 years earlier and smarter. Science as a weapon target.
items.tweetB: Buried in the SentinelOne teardown: fast16.sys was specifically targeting LS-DYNA, a simulation tool cited in reports on Iran's JCPOA nuclear violations. Someone was sabotaging Iran's ability to model nuclear weapons before Stuxnet even existed. The history of cyberwar is longer than we thought.

items.title: The Muon optimizer kills neurons — 1-in-4 dead by step 500; Aurora claims victory
items.idea: Tilde Research found Muon's update inherits row-norm anisotropy that causes MLP neurons to permanently die during learning rate warmup: 'More than one in four neurons are effectively dead' by step 500, producing a bimodal distribution of leverage scores. The new Aurora optimizer fixes this and achieves 2.26 vs 2.31 loss at 1.1B parameters, with +10 MMLU points over Muon — independently validated by Pleias.
items.angle: The optimizer wars matter because they directly affect model quality per dollar of compute. If Aurora generalizes, it could change training economics — and the question is whether it beats AdamW at scale.
items.tweetA: If you trained with Muon: 25% of your MLP neurons were dead by step 500 and never recovered. New paper from Tilde Research. Their fix, Aurora, gets +10 MMLU points over Muon on 1.1B params. The endless quest to beat AdamW has a new contender.
items.tweetB: The interesting thing about the Muon neuron death finding: it means many 'Muon-trained' models running in production right now have 25% of their MLP capacity doing essentially nothing. Not broken — just dead. How much capability is being left on the table?

items.title: Positive alignment: Oxford, OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind ask what AI success actually looks like
items.idea: A coalition from Oxford, Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, Stanford, UCLA and others published a position paper defining 'positive alignment' — building AI that not only avoids harm but actively supports human and ecological flourishing in a pluralistic, user-authored way. The argument: negative alignment (failure-mode reduction) is necessary but not sufficient. What does winning look like once you've solved safety?
items.angle: This framework will shape how frontier labs define their mission beyond harm avoidance — which has regulatory and commercial implications as governments ask what 'good AI' means.
items.tweetA: A question that rarely gets asked in AI: if we solve safety, then what? A paper from Oxford, OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, Stanford proposes 'positive alignment' — AI that actively helps people flourish, not just avoids harm. The hard part: defining flourishing without imposing one culture's values on everyone.
items.tweetB: Alignment research has spent a decade on 'don't break things.' The new frontier is 'make things actively good.' 10 institutions including all the major labs are now trying to formalize what that means. The answer will determine what these models are actually optimized for.

items.title: Bitcoin fails at 200-day MA — not just technical; macro backdrop turning
items.idea: Bitcoin bounced off but failed to close above the 200-day moving average last week, which on its own signals caution. But Ecoinometrics argues the more important issue is that BTC is not weakening in isolation — multiple rate-sensitive assets struggled simultaneously, suggesting the macro backdrop may be actively turning against the recovery rather than just a failed technical test.
items.angle: The 200-day MA failure combined with cross-asset rate sensitivity means this isn't a crypto-specific story — it's a rates story wearing a crypto costume.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin failed at the 200-day MA. That happens. What's different this week: it didn't fail in isolation. Multiple rate-sensitive assets cracked at the same time. This is rates repricing hitting BTC, not a BTC-specific event. The question is whether Hormuz resolves before the bond market prices in a hike.
items.tweetB: People ask why BTC is down. It's not complicated. US 10yr at 4.6%, 30yr at 5.13%. Market now pricing 60%+ chance of a Fed hike by December. BTC is a long-duration asset priced in USD. When the risk-free rate re-prices, so does everything else.

charts.desc: Charts inside showing BTC vs 200-day MA and cross-asset rate-sensitivity comparison

items.title: Iran seizes Chinese security vessel near Hormuz — same day as Trump-Xi summit
items.idea: Iran impounded a ship owned by Chinese security firm Sinoguards Marine Security on Thursday — the same day Trump met Xi Jinping in Beijing. Iran was granting passage to commercial ships while arresting the security vessel. Chatham House's Sanam Vakil: 'It is a way to remind the Chinese who is in control of Hormuz and they shouldn't even think of providing their own security.'
items.angle: This is Iran triangulating: letting commercial trade flow to signal goodwill while blocking Chinese security projection to maintain Hormuz leverage. A message sent directly to Beijing during the Trump-Xi meeting.
items.tweetA: Iran seized a Chinese security vessel near Hormuz on the same day Trump was meeting Xi in Beijing. Not an accident. Granting commercial passage but arresting the armed escort. Translation from Tehran: you can trade through here, but WE decide who provides security. China got the message during the summit.
items.tweetB: The Hormuz story is actually a China story. Iran just demonstrated it can veto Chinese security projection in the Gulf, even as it lets Chinese cargo through. That asymmetry — you can trade but not protect your ships — is exactly the leverage Iran needs to survive this conflict.

items.title: WHO declares Ebola outbreak global health emergency — Bundibugyo strain, no vaccine or treatment
items.idea: The WHO declared the ongoing Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda a public health emergency of international concern (PHEIC) on Sunday. The Bundibugyo ebolavirus strain — distinct from the 2014 Zaire strain — has no approved vaccine or treatment. WHO says it has been spreading undetected for weeks. CDC is escalating response. Risk to the US 'remains low' per CDC.
items.angle: Bundibugyo strain is the key detail — the 2014 vaccines (rVSV-ZEBOV) don't work against it. This is not the same playbook as 2014. Biotech angle: who's working on Bundibugyo-specific vaccines?
items.tweetA: WHO just declared the DRC/Uganda Ebola outbreak a global health emergency. The key detail everyone's missing: it's Bundibugyo strain. Not Zaire. The 2014 vaccines don't cover it. No approved treatment either. WHO says it's been spreading undetected for weeks. This is not the 2014 playbook.
items.tweetB: Ebola. WHO PHEIC declaration. Bundibugyo strain — which has no vaccine and no treatment. Spreading undetected in DRC and Uganda. CDC escalating. We have been here before and it went fine. We have also been here before and it did not.

items.title: China agrees $17B annual US agricultural purchases through 2028 — soybean commitments additive
items.idea: The White House announced China agreed to purchase at least $17 billion of US agricultural products annually through 2028, described as additive to soybean-purchase commitments made last fall. China's Commerce Ministry release was vaguer, noting 'series of measures' including mutually cutting levies — without providing specifics.
items.angle: The $17B number is a headline win for Trump but the specifics (what products, enforcement mechanism) are what matter for commodity markets. Boockvar separately flagged soybeans rebounding 2% on the China news.
items.tweetA: China committed to $17B/year in US agricultural purchases through 2028 per the Trump-Xi summit. On top of existing soybean commitments. Beijing's readout was notably vaguer — still negotiating details. The ag bull market thesis just got a demand-side catalyst. $SOYB $CORN
items.tweetB: The Trump-Xi summit produced: $17B/year China ag purchases, joint trade councils, and a Chinese readout that said 'we're still negotiating.' That gap between the White House fact sheet and Beijing's version is where all the actual risk lives.

items.title: Japan: Takaichi supplementary budget triggers fresh record highs in JGB super-long yields
items.idea: Japanese PM Takaichi called for an extra budget to respond to rising commodity prices — reversing prior insistence this wasn't necessary. Bloomberg: planned supplementary budget will fund emergency relief and require fresh debt issuance. Result: JGB 30yr +6bps to 4.10%, 40yr +9bps to 4.34%. Boockvar confirms. Takaichi also pushing back against conventional budget thinking while facing credibility questions.
items.angle: Japan is the canary in the global fiscal credibility mine. Each policy reversal that requires more bond issuance feeds a feedback loop: more issuance → higher yields → more cost of carry → more pressure to issue more. The 40yr at 4.34% is extraordinary.
items.tweetA: Japan's 40-year yield just hit 4.34%. PM Takaichi reversed her position and called for a supplementary budget — which means more JGB issuance. The BoJ is sitting on a mountain of bonds it bought to suppress yields. This is what fiscal dominance looks like: policy reversal → bond issuance → yield spike → repeat.
items.tweetB: The most important rate in the world right now might not be the US 30yr. It might be Japan's 40yr at 4.34%. When the country with the largest debt-to-GDP ratio among developed nations starts showing signs of fiscal credibility loss, the global carry trade unwinds.

items.title: UAE Barakah nuclear plant struck by drone — Saudi Arabia intercepts Iraqi drones same day
items.idea: A drone sparked a fire in a power station at UAE's Barakah nuclear plant over the weekend; UAE defense ministry confirmed it was launched from the west. Saudi Arabia separately intercepted three drones from Iraq (likely Iran-backed militia). The UAE has been described as 'the most aggressive Arab state toward Iran both in rhetoric and in limited attacks.' The truce began April 8, but strikes are continuing.
items.angle: Striking a nuclear power plant is a major escalation signal, even if only the power station (not the reactor) was hit. The Barakah plant being struck is a new category of target.
items.tweetA: A drone just hit the power station at UAE's Barakah nuclear plant. Not the reactor — but a nuclear facility just became a target. Saudi intercepted three Iraqi drones the same day. Iran's ceasefire (April 8) looks increasingly notional. The escalation ladder just gained a new rung.
items.tweetB: Everyone's focused on Hormuz. But the Barakah nuclear plant drone strike is the more important data point. Targeting nuclear infrastructure — even just the power station — is a signal about willingness to escalate across categories. Oil got the headline; this deserves more.

items.title: CLARITY Act passed Senate Banking Committee 15-9 — stablecoin yield resolved, DeFi exemption, token issuance pathway
items.idea: The CLARITY Act cleared Senate Banking Committee on May 14. Key provisions: (1) passive stablecoin interest banned, activity-linked rewards permitted; (2) token sales up to $50M/yr without SEC registration, $200M cumulative cap; (3) governance rights/staking alone cannot classify token as security; (4) sufficiently decentralized DeFi protocols exempt from SEC oversight; (5) decentralization now a legal prerequisite. Trump targeting July 4 signing.
items.angle: Tiger Research identifies 6 business models activated by this legislation. The activity-based reward mechanic is the key winner — it's a credit card cashback model funded by Treasury yield from stablecoin reserves.
items.tweetA: The CLARITY Act just cleared Senate Banking 15-9. The buried detail: 'sufficiently decentralized' is no longer a design philosophy — it's now a legal prerequisite for SEC exemption. Protocols with concentrated admin keys or upgrade authority are OUT. Decentralization just got real legal teeth. $HYPE $AAVE $UNI
items.tweetB: CLARITY Act: passive stablecoin yield banned, activity-based rewards permitted. Translation: Coinbase can't pay you 4% to hold USDC. But they CAN pay you rewards for trading, transacting, or staking. The credit card cashback model just came to crypto — funded by Treasury yield on stablecoin reserves.

items.title: Global bonds at multi-decade highs: JGB 10yr 19yr high, Bund 15yr high, Gilt 16yr high, US 30yr 19yr high
items.idea: Phil Dauber's morning note documents a synchronized global bond selloff: US 30yr at 5.13% (highest since June 2007), JGB 10yr at 19-year high of 2.80%, German Bund 10yr at 15-year high of 3.19%, UK Gilt 10yr at 16-year high of 5.19%. Brent crude >$110/barrel. Markets pricing 70% chance of Fed rate hike by December. Ed Yardeni: Fed should remove easing bias at June meeting.
items.angle: This is not a US-specific rates story. The synchronized global nature — DM bonds all at multi-decade highs simultaneously — suggests something structural is happening beyond just Iran/oil. Fiscal credibility + energy shock + AI capex all at once.
items.tweetA: Everyone's watching the US 30yr at 5.13%. But the global picture is scarier. JGB 10yr: 19-year high. German Bund: 15-year high. UK Gilt: 16-year high. All of them. Simultaneously. This is not an Iran war story — this is a fiscal credibility story using the war as the trigger.
items.tweetB: Philly Fed now forecasting Q2 inflation at 6%. Markets pricing 70% chance of Fed hike by December. US 30yr at a 19-year high. Yardeni: remove easing bias at June. Bessent: 'transient.' We've had 62 consecutive months above the Fed's 2% target. At what point does 'transient' get retired?

items.title: Philly Fed: Q2 inflation forecast 6% — 62 consecutive months above 2% target
items.idea: The Philadelphia Fed's latest quarterly Survey of Professional Forecasters projects Q2 2026 inflation at 6%, with long-term inflation expected to remain above 2%. Bessent calls energy-driven inflation 'a short-term blip.' 62 consecutive months of inflation above the Fed's 2% target. The duration argument against transitory is now measured in years.
items.angle: The contrast between Bessent's 'transient' framing and Philly Fed's 6% Q2 forecast is the central tension in rates right now. If inflation expectations become unmoored, this is the data point to watch.
items.tweetA: Philly Fed Survey of Professional Forecasters: Q2 inflation 6%. Bessent: it's transient. We have been 62 months above 2%. At what point does 'transient' just mean 'we don't want to admit it's structural'?
items.tweetB: The math on 'transient inflation': 62 consecutive months above target. Current Q2 forecast: 6%. Markets pricing 70% hike odds. Either Bessent is right and Hormuz resolves fast. Or the Fed's next move is up, not down. No middle ground much longer.

items.title: Silver collapses 11% to $76/oz — severe deleveraging as safe havens dump alongside risk assets
items.idea: Spot silver collapsed over 11% to around $76/oz in what Dauber describes as 'severe deleveraging.' Cross-asset correlations are breaking down — traditional safe havens like gold and silver are selling off alongside risk assets. This is characteristic of forced liquidation when all correlations go to 1.
items.angle: Silver at -11% in a single session is a significant tell. When silver dumps with equities, it often means someone big is being margin-called — not a directional market view. Watch for forced selling cascading.
items.tweetA: Silver -11% today to $76/oz. Not because anyone hated silver. Because when you need to raise cash fast, you sell what's liquid. Cross-asset correlations going to 1 is the signature of a forced deleveraging event, not a market view. Watch who else is selling things they don't want to sell.
items.tweetB: Safe haven assets dumping alongside risk is the tell. Gold and silver down with equities means the flight-to-safety bid is overwhelmed by forced selling. Someone — or multiple someones — is being margin-called. This ends when the deleveraging ends, not when the fundamentals improve.

items.title: Pakistan deploys fighter jet squadron + 8,000 troops to Saudi Arabia under defense pact
items.idea: Reuters reports Pakistan has deployed a fighter jet squadron, around 8,000 troops, and air defense systems to Saudi Arabia under an existing defense pact. KSA is building defensive capacity as the Iran conflict intensifies. Pakistan-Saudi military cooperation under a bilateral framework — significance: draws a non-Arab Muslim nuclear state into the regional conflict.
items.angle: Pakistan's troop deployment to Saudi Arabia triangulates multiple vectors: Pakistan borders both Iran and India, has nuclear weapons, and is now openly aligned with Saudi on the Gulf conflict. The regional coalition against Iran is broadening.
items.tweetA: Under-covered: Pakistan just deployed a fighter jet squadron, 8,000 troops, and air defense systems to Saudi Arabia under their defense pact. A Muslim-majority nuclear power moving military assets to the Persian Gulf. The regional coalition against Iran is getting more explicit.
items.tweetB: Pakistan borders both Iran and India. Has nuclear weapons. Just deployed major military assets to Saudi Arabia. This is no longer just a US-Iran or Israel-Iran story. The regional alignment is hardening fast.

items.title: Ackman builds $2.1B Microsoft stake — called it 'two of the most valuable franchises in enterprise tech'
items.idea: Pershing Square has built a $2.1 billion stake in Microsoft, taking advantage of the company's share price decline. Ackman described Microsoft 365 and Azure as 'two of the most valuable franchises in enterprise technology.'
items.angle: Ackman going long MSFT at this size is a high-conviction macro call embedded in a specific stock: betting that AI-driven Azure growth + 365 stickiness outweighs current headwinds from rates and market multiple compression.
items.tweetA: Pershing Square built a $2.1B Microsoft position. Ackman's thesis in one line: 365 and Azure are two of the most valuable franchises in enterprise tech. At current prices after the drawdown, that's a high-conviction bet that the AI cycle is durable regardless of what happens to multiples near-term. $MSFT
items.tweetB: I'm less interested in Ackman's Microsoft thesis and more interested in what it means that a concentrated value fund is making a $2.1B bet on an AI infrastructure play right now — while rates are spiking and bonds are at 19-year highs. That's a bet that the AI buildout continues even if rates stay high.

items.title: Japan 30yr yield hits 4.10%, 40yr hits 4.34% — US 30yr at 19-year high 5.13%; Boockvar cites fiscal credibility
items.idea: Boockvar frames the global bond selloff as 'debts and deficits now matter' — not just oil/Iran. Japan's Takaichi government requesting a supplementary budget moved JGB 30yr +6bps to 4.10% and 40yr +9bps to 4.34%. This is feeding into the US 30yr at a 19-year high of 5.13%.
items.angle: The thesis Boockvar has held for years — excessive debts and deficits will eventually demand a risk premium — is now manifesting simultaneously across DM sovereign debt. This is a regime change.
items.tweetA: Peter Boockvar's thesis for years: debts and deficits will eventually matter. Today's data: Japan 40yr at 4.34%. US 30yr at 5.13%, 19-year high. The trigger was Takaichi calling for more bond issuance. But the thesis was always right — the trigger was just waiting to arrive.
items.tweetB: Japan's 40yr bond yield at 4.34% is not an oil story. It's a fiscal credibility story. When a government that was insisting no extra budget was needed reverses — within weeks — bond markets notice. The same dynamic is playing across every DM sovereign. $TBT $TMV

charts.desc: Charts of JGB 30yr yield and US 30yr yield — both breaking out. CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Baker Hughes rig count +5 to 415 — still 82 below two years ago; early ag bull market thesis
items.idea: US crude oil rig count rose 5 to 415 (most since November 2025), but remains well below 465 a year ago and 497 two years ago. Despite Brent >$110, capital discipline and cost inflation are constraining the supply response. Boockvar notes soybeans rebounding 2% on Chinese purchases and argues for an early-stage ag bull market alongside industrial metals, precious metals, and energy.
items.angle: The rig count gap — 82 below two years ago despite $110 oil — is the structural undersupply story in energy. Capital discipline from E&P management + ESG pressure + cost inflation means the supply response to high prices is historically slow.
items.tweetA: Brent >$110. US oil rig count: 415. Two years ago at similar prices: 497 rigs. The shale supply response to $110 oil is 82 rigs short of where it was. Capital discipline, cost inflation, ESG pressure — producers aren't drilling like it's 2022. This is why the energy trade has more room. $XOM $XLE
items.tweetB: Boockvar: ag bull market joining industrial metals, precious metals, and energy. China buying $17B/yr in US ag. Soybeans +2% today. Rig count still lagging despite $110 oil. The commodity supercycle thesis isn't dead — it just needed a geopolitical catalyst to make itself visible.

charts.desc: Chart of crude oil rig count showing the gap below prior year levels

items.title: Hyperliquid oil futures: $339M to $7.3B in 2 weeks; CME & ICE lobbying CFTC to kill it
items.idea: Hyperliquid's oil perpetual futures volume grew from $339M cumulative in late February to $7.3B by March 12 — driven by Iran war hedging demand. The exchange now controls 34-44% of the decentralized derivatives market. CME and ICE are privately lobbying the CFTC and Congress, claiming Hyperliquid's anonymous trading could allow sanctions circumvention in oil markets.
items.angle: CME and ICE going to CFTC is the classic incumbent regulatory capture move. The irony: Hyperliquid is MORE transparent than traditional dark pools, with every trade onchain in real time. The lobbying tells you TradFi is scared.
items.tweetA: CME and ICE are lobbying the CFTC to regulate Hyperliquid out of oil derivatives. The reason they give: anonymous trading could circumvent sanctions. The real reason: Hyperliquid went from $339M to $7.3B in oil futures volume in 2 weeks. Incumbents lobby when they're scared, not when they're confident.
items.tweetB: Hyperliquid's defense against CME/ICE lobbying: every transaction published onchain in real time. Compare that to the opacity in traditional derivatives markets. They're calling HYPE a transparency risk. HYPE publishes everything. Who's actually the black box here?

items.title: SpaceX pre-IPO debuts on Hyperliquid at $208 — $40M volume in 12 hours; potential $2T IPO
items.idea: SpaceX launched for pre-IPO trading on Hyperliquid's HIP-3 platform overnight, trading at $208 per share with $40M in volume in its first 12 hours. Arkham estimates SpaceX could IPO at $2T valuation. Kalshi gives 70% odds of Tesla/SpaceX merger. Cerebras previously debuted on Nasdaq after Hyperliquid was the home for pre-IPO price discovery.
items.angle: If SpaceX sees strong pre-IPO demand at $208 on Hyperliquid, it provides a public price anchor for the $2T IPO narrative. The pre-IPO discovery function is the most threatening thing Hyperliquid does to incumbents.
items.tweetA: SpaceX just went live on Hyperliquid for pre-IPO trading: $208/share, $40M volume in 12 hours. Arkham: $2T potential IPO valuation. Kalshi: 70% odds of Tesla/SpaceX merger. The price discovery is happening on a decentralized exchange. That's what's terrifying CME and ICE. $HYPE
items.tweetB: The SpaceX pre-IPO on Hyperliquid is a stress test for the whole pre-IPO discovery thesis. Cerebras was the proof of concept. SpaceX is the first truly massive test — a company potentially going public at $2T. If HYPE handles it, every future pre-IPO will start there first.

items.title: Trump family filed 3,600+ crypto transactions Q1 2026 — $51M personal digital asset stake, during CLARITY Act markup
items.idea: Trump's family trust filed ethics disclosures covering 3,600+ securities transactions from January–March 2026, including 9 Coinbase purchases, 2 MARA acquisitions, 8 Strategy transactions, and positions in Block, Robinhood, SoFi. Trump's personal digital asset stake is at least $51M. 70 Trump administration officials collectively hold over $193M in crypto.
items.angle: The ethics exclusion language — whether officials can profit from crypto they're regulating — is the make-or-break provision for bipartisan CLARITY Act support.
items.tweetA: The Trump family filed 3,600+ crypto-related securities transactions while the administration was writing crypto legislation. $51M personal digital asset stake. 70 officials hold $193M in crypto collectively. The CLARITY Act ethics provision is the fulcrum. Watch that language.
items.tweetB: Coinbase, MARA, Strategy, Robinhood — the Trump family bought them all in Q1 while the CLARITY Act was being drafted. I don't know if that's legal. But I know it's the exact reason the Democratic objections to the bill are about ethics, not policy.

items.title: BofA NACHO rates: 'Not A Chance Hormuz Opens' — pay US front end, long 10yr Bund vs 10yr UST
items.idea: BofA's Global Rates Weekly coined NACHO (Not A Chance Hormuz Opens). Their positioning: pay US front end & June '26 SONIA, long 10Y Bund outright and vs 10Y UST. Thesis: US inflation beats + higher oil point to underpriced hike risk. Revised 2yr and 5yr yield forecasts higher through H1 2027.
items.angle: NACHO is the framework trade: if you believe Hormuz stays blocked, front-end rates must price more hike risk. The Bund-vs-UST spread trade captures US-specific fiscal/inflation premium over German fiscal conservatism.
items.tweetA: BofA coined the trade of the moment: NACHO — Not A Chance Hormuz Opens. Positioning: pay US front end, pay Jun '26 SONIA, long 10Y Bund vs 10Y UST. Translation: the US is pricing insufficient hike risk while Europe prices conservatism. The spread trades the gap.
items.tweetB: NACHO rates framework: Hormuz stays closed → oil elevated → US inflation beats → front-end rates underprice hike risk. If you believe the war drags, you're paying 2yr rates and watching for the moment 'cuts are coming' flips to 'cuts are wrong.' That moment may already be here.

items.title: G7 finance ministers Paris: implied G7 long-bond borrowing costs highest in 24 years
items.idea: G7 finance ministers gathered in Paris acknowledging mounting concern over public debt and bond market volatility. Implied borrowing costs from indexes of G7 government bonds with maturities 10yr+ have risen to their highest in 24 years. 30yr US Treasuries at loftiest levels since before the 2007-08 banking crash. France seeking G7 consensus that US deficit and China trade surplus represent growth risks.
items.angle: 24-year high in G7 long-bond costs is the coordinated fiscal credibility signal. When every developed government is paying 24-year highs to borrow, 'fiscal space' becomes fiction.
items.tweetA: G7 finance ministers in Paris today. The number that matters: implied borrowing costs on G7 government bonds with 10yr+ maturities just hit a 24-year high. Not a coincidence they're meeting. When every developed government is paying 24-year highs to borrow, fiscal space becomes fiction.
items.tweetB: France wants G7 to acknowledge US deficit and China trade surplus as systemic risks. Bond market is having the conversation anyway — 30yr Treasuries at pre-2008 highs. The Paris meeting is the policy response to a crisis the market declared without waiting for a vote.

items.title: Barclays: 3 scenarios for a 2026 Fed hike — market now pricing 60%+ probability by December
items.idea: Barclays outlines three scenarios for an FOMC hike: (1) inflation expectations unmooring in 5-10yr market measures; (2) core PCE persistently above target even as tariff effects fade; (3) demand outpacing supply — growth above potential, unemployment below NAIRU, wage growth reaccelerating. Barclays baseline: extended hold then 25bp cut March 2027. Markets: 60%+ hike odds by December.
items.angle: Scenario 1 (expectations unmooring) is the most dangerous because it's self-reinforcing. Once long-run expectations move, the Fed has to hike aggressively to recapture credibility.
items.tweetA: Barclays outlines what triggers a 2026 Fed hike. #1: inflation expectations unmooring in 5-10yr measures. That's the nuclear scenario — once long-run expectations move, the Fed has to hike aggressively to recapture credibility. The line from Hormuz to 1970s-style stagflation runs through this channel.
items.tweetB: The market is pricing 60%+ chance of a Fed hike this year. Barclays baseline doesn't include one. That gap is where trades are made. Either the market is wrong and you fade it. Or Barclays revises by June. Watching FOMC Minutes Wednesday for clues.

items.title: Bond seasonality: historically bullish April–August — contrarian signal; 5yr stochastics oversold
items.idea: Bond Beat notes historically (2010-2019 expansion), 10yr yields tended to fall during April-August relative to trend and rise September-March. Bond seasonal tailwinds are now active — which is contrarian to current events. 5yr stochastic momentum is now 'firmly oversold' suggesting a short-term tactical buying opportunity.
items.angle: The seasonality argument is a tactical counterweight to the structural bear thesis. If Hormuz resolves by summer, seasonal + oversold stochastic combo creates a mean-reversion trade.
items.tweetA: Bond seasonality says April-August is the historically friendly window for Treasuries. The 5yr is technically oversold per stochastics. Everything else in the macro backdrop says sell bonds. The trade question: is this a temporary dislocation you fade tactically, or the moment the historical pattern breaks permanently?
items.tweetB: Every seasonal, every technical signal says the bond selloff is overdone short-term. The structural picture says yields go higher. These two views can both be right — bounce to 4.30% on 10s, then new highs. The traders who get hurt are the ones who confuse a tactical bounce with a trend reversal.

items.title: Empire Manufacturing: 19.6 vs 7.0 consensus — best in 4+ years; pull-forward or genuine?
items.idea: The NY Fed Empire Manufacturing index jumped from 11 to 19.6 in May, its best result in more than four years, crushing the consensus expectation of 7.0. DiMartino Booth frames the key question: is this driven by pull-ahead behavior (ordering ahead of expected tariff escalation or supply disruptions) or a genuine rekindling of industrial activity?
items.angle: The pull-forward vs. genuine debate is the lens for reading all industrial data right now. A 19.6 reading that proves to be pull-forward sets up a sharp reversal in subsequent months.
items.tweetA: Empire Manufacturing just printed 19.6 vs a consensus of 7.0 — best in over 4 years. DiMartino Booth's question is the right one: pull-forward ahead of tariff/supply disruptions, or genuine industrial rekindling? One of these leads to a hangover in Q3. The answer determines the rest of the year.
items.tweetB: The best Empire Manufacturing reading in 4 years — in the middle of $110 oil, 5% 30yr yields, and a Middle East war. Either manufacturers are front-running supply disruptions, or the AI capex cycle is sustaining industrial demand despite everything else. I'd bet on pull-forward until proved otherwise.

items.title: Iran's 'Hormuz Safe' system to settle in Bitcoin — $4,540 gold, $102 oil, Fear & Greed: 28
items.idea: Mando Minutes reports Iran plans a new Strait of Hormuz transit system called 'Hormuz Safe' that would settle payments in Bitcoin. Gold $4,540, oil $102, BTC Fear & Greed at 28 (fear territory). BTC ETF -$290M daily, ETH ETF -$66M.
items.angle: If oil tanker transit fees are settled in BTC, this is the first major real-world state-level use case for Bitcoin as energy-trade settlement infrastructure — beyond narrative.
items.tweetA: Iran is reportedly planning to settle Hormuz transit fees in Bitcoin. If true, this is the first sovereign use of BTC as energy-trade settlement infrastructure. Not as a store of value. As a payment rail for oil shipping in a sanctions regime. This is the use case nobody was modeling.
items.tweetB: BTC is down 7% this week on rising yields and ETF outflows. Also this week: Iran may settle Strait of Hormuz transit fees in BTC. Hayes lowered his target to $125k. BTC long-term holders at a 1-year high. The narrative and the price are going in opposite directions right now.

items.title: Goldman Sachs: central banks to buy 60 tonnes of gold per month in 2026
items.idea: Goldman Sachs projects central bank gold purchases at 60 tonnes per month in 2026. Gold is currently at $4,540/oz. Central bank buying has been the structural bid under gold since 2022, accelerating as de-dollarization themes intensify amid US sanctions use, dollar weaponization fears, and the Hormuz/Iran conflict.
items.angle: 60T/month is roughly 720T/year — significantly above historical norms. If this forecast holds, the structural central bank bid provides a floor that makes gold dips shallow even as rates rise.
items.tweetA: Goldman: central banks buying 60 tonnes of gold per month in 2026. That's 720 tonnes/year — well above historical norms. Gold at $4,540. The structural bid from central banks is not a thesis anymore. It's a policy. And it's happening while everyone argues about whether BTC is digital gold. $GLD $PHYS
items.tweetB: Central banks buying gold at 60T/month per Goldman. Meanwhile sovereign wealth funds are diversifying away from Treasuries. The dollar is still the reserve currency, but gold is quietly becoming the settlement layer for countries that don't trust the dollar system.

items.title: SpaceX $2T IPO thesis; Tesla/SpaceX merger 70% on Kalshi; Figure F.03 robot beats human in 8-hour shift
items.idea: Three technology signals: (1) Arkham projects SpaceX could IPO at $2T valuation; (2) Kalshi prediction market gives 70% probability to Tesla/SpaceX merger; (3) Figure Robotics' F.03 robot beat a human in an 8-hour operational shift test — a milestone in humanoid productivity at scale.
items.angle: The Tesla/SpaceX merger at 70% is a major narrative for TSLA multiples and standalone SpaceX IPO. Figure F.03 beating a human in an 8-hour shift is the inflection point where humanoid robotics becomes economically viable.
items.tweetA: Three signals from this morning: (1) Kalshi 70% odds on Tesla/SpaceX merger. (2) Arkham: SpaceX $2T IPO. (3) Figure F.03 robot beat a human in an 8-hour factory shift. Not a demo. An operational test. We're debating whether AI is overhyped while robots are passing 8-hour productivity tests.
items.tweetB: Figure F.03 beat a human in an 8-hour factory shift. This is where the humanoid robotics investment thesis stops being abstract. The question is now when, not if. $TSLA

items.title: OpenAI expects $600B compute spend to 2030; Memory now 36% of hyperscaler capex; CoreWeave $100B backlog
items.idea: Three AI infrastructure data points: (1) OpenAI forecasting $600B in total compute spending through 2030; (2) Memory represents ~36% of hyperscaler capex; (3) CoreWeave has a $100B revenue backlog. DRAM/NAND demand now >50% of total memory TAM.
items.angle: The memory capex shift (36% of hyperscaler spend) is the tell for which semiconductor segments benefit most from AI buildout — not just GPUs. DRAM and NAND suppliers are the hidden winners.
items.tweetA: Memory is now 36% of hyperscaler capex. DRAM/NAND demand is over 50% of total memory TAM. OpenAI sees $600B in compute spending through 2030. CoreWeave has $100B in backlog. The AI buildout is a memory buildout. Everyone watches GPU. The real trade might be HBM and NAND. $MU
items.tweetB: CoreWeave: $100B revenue backlog. OpenAI: $600B compute spend to 2030. Memory: 36% of hyperscaler capex. These are not venture capital projections. They are contracted revenue and capex budgets. The infrastructure side of AI is not speculative anymore.

items.title: Chamath calls Anthropic 'AI's Friendster'; Microsoft: white-collar automation in 18 months; Citadel: AI agents doing PhD finance work
items.idea: Three AI adoption/competition signals: (1) Chamath publicly called Anthropic 'AI's Friendster' — suggesting displacement risk; (2) Microsoft says white-collar work will be fully automated by AI within 18 months; (3) Citadel CEO says AI agents are now doing PhD-level finance work.
items.angle: Chamath's Friendster call is a provocation worth taking seriously — Anthropic is the safety-focused lab potentially losing ground to OpenAI's product velocity. If it becomes the 'good values' lab without a dominant product, the comparison holds.
items.tweetA: Chamath just called Anthropic 'AI's Friendster.' Remember Friendster? It built the social graph before Facebook. First-mover, good product, then completely displaced. Is Anthropic being outrun on product velocity while obsessing on alignment? That's the bear case in one cultural reference.
items.tweetB: Citadel CEO says AI agents are now doing PhD finance work. Microsoft says white-collar jobs automated in 18 months. Chamath calls Anthropic 'Friendster.' The AI cycle is compressing: from 'AI can do this' to 'AI is doing this' to 'humans aren't needed for this' faster than the labor market can absorb.

items.title: Token budget explosion: Uber's 5,000 engineers depleted entire 2026 AI budget in 4 months; AI cost shifts from fixed to variable
items.idea: Uber's CTO reported 5,000 engineers depleted their entire 2026 token budget by April (4 months in). ServiceNow similarly blew past budgets. 71% of companies exceeded AI budgets in 2025. Average monthly AI spend by large US enterprises grew 36% to $85K between 2024 and 2025. Exponential View's key framing: AI cost is no longer fixed (like SaaS seat licenses) but variable (like a utility).
items.angle: The shift from fixed to variable cost is the hidden CFO story of 2026. When AI was a $20/month Copilot seat, it was predictable. Now it's a usage-based model that 5,000 engineers can blow through in 4 months. Enterprise AI spend is massively understated in current consensus.
items.tweetA: Uber CTO: 5,000 engineers burned through the entire 2026 AI token budget in 4 months. ServiceNow same. AI moved from a fixed SaaS cost (predictable, budgetable) to a variable utility cost (unbounded, demand-driven). 71% of companies already exceeded AI budgets in 2025. Consensus is underestimating enterprise AI spend.
items.tweetB: The token budget explosion is a demand signal. When engineers consume AI faster than CFOs can budget, productivity is real. The question: can AI vendors lower per-token cost fast enough to stay below the budget ceiling? If not, the ceiling becomes the growth constraint.

charts.desc: Charts inside showing enterprise AI spend growth and budget overruns

items.title: Post-OpEx gamma vacuum: $2.25T expired Friday — can enter negative gamma this week; 7,293 is the level
items.idea: Monthly OpEx cleared $2.25T in SPY/SPX open interest on Friday. The SPX gamma flip sits at 7,309 — just 16 points below Friday's close of 7,409. This is the first time the flip line is inside the weekly expected move (7,293 to 7,524). A close below 7,293 would push SPX into negative gamma, where dealer hedging amplifies rather than suppresses moves.
items.angle: The gamma setup is bearish this week: positive gamma support evaporated with OpEx, the flip is inside the weekly range, and NVIDIA Wednesday is the wild card. A NVDA miss + 7,293 breach = violent move lower.
items.tweetA: $2.25 trillion in S&P options expired last Friday. The gamma flip that has been suppressing vol is at 7,309 — 100 points below Friday's close. This is the first week the flip is inside the weekly expected move. NVDA reports Wednesday. If NVDA misses and we break 7,293, dealers go from suppressing moves to amplifying them. Watch the level.
items.tweetB: The market's mechanical support just evaporated: $2.25T in OpEx gamma rolled off Friday. Gamma flip at 7,309. Weekly range: 7,293 to 7,524. NVIDIA Wednesday. Post-OpEx is the 3-5 day window when price discovery gets honest. This week is that window.

items.title: Energy (XLE) +6.7% vs XLK flat last week — stark rotation on Friday as energy was only green sector
items.idea: XLE posted roughly +6.7% on the 5-day window with a positive 1-day return of +2.1% on Friday — the only sector with a positive 1-day return when everything else sold off. XLK was essentially flat on the week and down ~2% on Friday. If NVIDIA delivers Wednesday, XLK reasserts leadership and the rotation reverses. If NVIDIA disappoints, the rotation into energy and defensives has more room.
items.angle: The XLE-vs-XLK divergence on a single session is the clearest signal of the emerging regime change: from AI-multiple-expansion to energy-geopolitical-premium. One day doesn't make a trend but the divergence was stark.
items.tweetA: Friday: XLE +2.1%. Every other sector: negative. This is what it looks like when a market starts rotating from 'AI growth' to 'energy reality.' XLK was -2% on the day. If NVIDIA misses Wednesday, this rotation has legs. If NVIDIA delivers, it was a one-day blip. That's the binary for the week.
items.tweetB: The sector chart that matters: XLE +6.7% for the week. XLK flat. On a single session Friday, every other sector sold while energy bid. Oil at $102, 30yr yields at 5.13%, geopolitical premium expanding. Does NVIDIA's print reset this or confirm it?

charts.desc: 5-day momentum chart showing XLE divergence from all other sectors on Friday — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Vol-control funds: $200B deployed vs $300B AUM cap — 1/3 dry powder; CTA rebuilt from bear lows to 1.145
items.idea: Vol-control funds bought $5.4B over the past 10 days, current implied allocation at ~$200B against estimated $300B AUM cap. At 2021 peak and early 2024, allocations ran near $275-300B. CTAs rebuilt positioning from near-2022-bear-market lows to 1.145. At 1-month realized vol of 11.35%, the algo still points toward elevated equity exposure.
items.angle: One-third of vol-control capacity still undeployed is a structural tailwind IF low-vol regime holds. If NVIDIA misses and vol spikes, the same algo mechanically sells. The vol regime is the on/off switch.
items.tweetA: Vol-control funds have deployed $200B of a potential $300B. The remaining $100B flows in automatically if realized volatility stays low. 1-month RV is 11.35%. The low-vol regime is the switch: it stays on → $100B more equities. It turns off → systematic selling starts. NVDA Wednesday is the test.
items.tweetB: CTAs rebuilt positioning from near-2022 bear market lows to 1.145. Four consecutive buying days. The structural systematic bid has been real. At these levels, CTAs have less buying capacity remaining than they've already deployed. The easy phase of the rebuild is done.

items.title: NVIDIA + FOMC Minutes Wednesday; heavy macro May 28 (±2.79%); SPX weekly expected move 7,293 to 7,524
items.idea: Gamma Report maps the event calendar: NVIDIA after close Wednesday is the dominant event. Home Depot Tuesday and Walmart Thursday bracket it with consumer reads. FOMC Minutes Wednesday. Heavier macro May 28: Core PCE, GDP, Durable Goods, Personal Income, Personal Spending. SPX IV term structure pricing event risk in measured upward slope — May 28 expiration implies ±2.79%.
items.angle: NVDA Wednesday + FOMC Minutes in a single session creates a cross-asset binary: NVDA beats/Minutes hawkish (cancel out) vs NVDA miss/Minutes hawkish (everything sells together). Options market is pricing this as manageable; I'm not sure they've priced the combination.
items.tweetA: Wednesday is the single most data-dense day of the week: NVIDIA earnings + FOMC Minutes. Either these cancel each other out (NVDA beats, Minutes hawkish) or they compound (NVDA miss + hawkish Minutes). The SPX weekly expected move is just ±1.6%. I'm not sure the options market has priced the combination.
items.tweetB: Gamma Report's May 28 data point: Core PCE, GDP, Durable Goods, PCE Price Index, Personal Income, Personal Spending — all in one window. SPX IV pricing ±2.79% for that date. Wednesday is the warm-up act. May 28 is the real test.

items.title: UK political risk rising: Andy Burnham by-election June 18 — sterling put/call ratio 6:1
items.idea: With Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham seeking a Parliamentary seat in a June 18 by-election — potentially setting up a Labour leadership challenge — sterling put options vs dollar volume was more than six times larger than calls last week. Risk reversals printed at their most euro-bullish since April 2025. Burnham walked back comments about the UK being 'in hock to bond markets' while committing to fiscal rules.
items.angle: The UK fiscal credibility thread: 10yr gilt at 5.12%, Starmer refusing to set departure timeline, Burnham clarifying bond market comments, sterling puts 6x calls. Early-stage political risk premia building in a market not expecting UK leadership disruption.
items.tweetA: UK: 10yr gilt at 5.12% (16-year high). Starmer refusing to set departure timeline. Andy Burnham by-election June 18 with leadership ambitions. Sterling put volume 6x call volume this week. Risk reversals most EUR-bullish since April 2025. The market is starting to price UK political instability.
items.tweetB: The sterling put/call ratio (6:1) is telling you something. Burnham said the UK was 'in hock to bond markets' then walked it back. When a PM-in-waiting sounds skeptical of fiscal rules and gilts are at 16-year highs, the FX market hedges first and asks questions later.

items.title: BTC ETFs bled $1B in net outflows last week — Harvard cuts IBIT 43%; SOL/XRP ETFs see inflows
items.idea: Bitcoin spot ETFs saw nearly $1B in net outflows across the past week, snapping a 6-week inflow streak. Today alone: BTC ETF -$290M, ETH ETF -$66M. BlackRock has apparently become a net seller. Harvard University cut its IBIT (BlackRock BTC ETF) position by 43% and completely exited its ETH ETF holding. SOL and XRP ETFs are seeing inflows even as BTC and ETH see outflows — a rotation signal within crypto.
items.angle: Harvard cutting IBIT by 43% is an institutional sentiment signal. When a major endowment trims after 6 weeks of institutional inflows, it's not panic — it's de-risking at levels they didn't anticipate reaching.
items.tweetA: Harvard cut its IBIT position 43% and fully exited ETH ETF. BTC ETFs bleeding $1B net outflows last week. BlackRock apparently a net seller. The institutional narrative that drove 6 weeks of ETF inflows just reversed. Harvard exiting is not a retail decision — it's a portfolio manager derisking ahead of rising rates.
items.tweetB: SOL and XRP ETFs: inflows. BTC and ETH ETFs: outflows. Harvard cuts IBIT 43%. This is institutional rotation within crypto, not capitulation. The question: are they rotating into SOL/XRP because of fundamentals (Firedancer live, CLARITY Act) or because they need liquidity from BTC/ETH to fund something else?

items.title: Firedancer goes live on Solana mainnet; Verus-Ethereum bridge drained $11.4M; Bitcoin Depot Chapter 11
items.idea: Three crypto infrastructure signals: (1) Firedancer validator client is live on Solana mainnet — a major infrastructure upgrade that enables higher throughput and reduces single-client risk; (2) Verus-Ethereum bridge was exploited for $11.4M; (3) Bitcoin Depot (BTM operator) filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy. VVV hit 3M users, HYPE at local high $47 after Coinbase partnership.
items.angle: Firedancer going live is an underappreciated Solana upgrade — first independent validator client, meaningfully reduces single-point-of-failure risk. Bitcoin Depot's Chapter 11 reflects BTM operators squeezed as crypto adoption goes digital and makes physical ATMs less necessary.
items.tweetA: Firedancer just went live on Solana mainnet. This isn't marketing. It's the first independent validator client for Solana, reducing single-client failure risk that crashed the network before. Meanwhile VVV hit 3M users and HYPE is at $47. Solana's infrastructure is maturing while everyone watches BTC price. $SOL
items.tweetB: Bitcoin Depot filed Chapter 11 this week. Bitcoin ATM operators getting squeezed: declining cash-based BTC usage as adoption goes digital, rising compliance costs. The irony: the ATM business was built for people who couldn't access crypto through banks. The banks now offer crypto.

items.title: Phase 3 basket: short-momentum names showing institutional accumulation in 13F data
items.idea: Lord Fed notes that 13F filings show noticeable institutional buying in software names that are part of his Phase 3 basket — names that are effectively short-momentum plays. These are accumulating while the broader momentum rally extends. Phase 3 names rally when momentum comes off because they are the inverse trade to the crowded long momentum position.
items.angle: The accumulation signal in Phase 3 names is the key tell: institutional investors are quietly building the counter-trade while the momentum crowd is capitulating into longs. When the reflexive bid turns, Phase 3 is already positioned.
items.tweetA: 13F data showing institutional accumulation in software names that are anti-momentum trades. While the discretionary crowd capitulates and buys the rally, smart money is quietly building the counter-position. Phase 3: the names that rally when momentum comes off.
items.tweetB: The most interesting positioning signal: institutional buys in software names that are short-momentum plays. Someone is building the reversal trade while everyone else chases the move. That's either very wrong or very early. Given the reflexive bid mechanics, I know which side I want to be on.
